tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.title: Slow data structures access

hint.workaround:
Use vector aligned instructions:

 2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: read your compiler manual.

The binary loop is loading 616 bytes (77 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 32 bytes (4 double precision FP elements).

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 27.67 cycles. At this rate:
 - 23% of peak load performance is reached (22.27 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 1% of peak store performance is reached (1.16 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks
expert.txt:
Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 27.67 to 25.33 cycles (1.09x speedup).

header: 10% of peak computational performance is used (2.60 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround: Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.
gain.details:
49% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 21% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 41% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is poorly vectorized.
Only 27% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).

gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt: Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.

potential.title: 512-bits vectorization
potential.txt: On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.

potential.workaround:
Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
Estimated speedup by perfect pairing: 1.01x.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).
potential.title: FMA
potential.txt:
Detected 12 FMA (fused multiply-add) operations.
Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.
